did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Light GBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) and a deep learning architecture (LesNet-22, based on CNN and ANN) for the classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2019 datasets. It also employs Faster-RCNN for lesion detection and GrabCut for segmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the proposal of a novel deep learning architecture (LesNet-22) and the application of machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Light GBM, etc.) for skin lesion classification. It details specific methodologies (preprocessing, segmentation with GrabCut, detection with Faster-RCNN), describes the datasets used (ISIC-2017, ISIC-2019), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC). Although published in a conference (INDICON), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparative Analysis of Skin Lesions Classification Using Machine Learning Classifiers and Lesnet-22 Architecture
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-assisted approach for classifying skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2019 datasets. The methodology includes preprocessing with the Dull Razor algorithm, lesion detection via Faster-RCNN, and segmentation using GrabCut. Classification is performed using both machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Light GBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) and a novel deep learning architecture named LesNet-22. The LesNet-22 model achieved the highest performance with 94% accuracy on ISIC-2017 and 91% accuracy on ISIC-2019, outperforming the machine learning classifiers. The results demonstrate that the proposed deep learning framework effectively classifies skin lesions with high accuracy and AUC scores.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in both ISIC-2017 (3-class subset) and ISIC-2019 (8-class subset) datasets.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, nevus, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the ISIC-2017 (3-class: MEL, SK, NV) and ISIC-2019 (8-class: AKEIC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NEV, SCC, VASC) datasets. Methods include machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Light GBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) and a deep learning architecture (LesNet-22). Performance is measured by accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.
The paper employs Faster-RCNN for automated skin lesion localization/detection, reporting accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for this detection task.
The paper uses the GrabCut algorithm for semi-automatic segmentation of skin lesions to separate foreground (affected) from background (healthy) regions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) challenge, consisting of dermoscopic images. The study used a subset of this dataset for classification.

use.training: Used for training machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Light GBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) and the LesNet-22 deep learning model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the LesNet-22 model (accuracy graphs shown).
use.testing: Used for testing and performance evaluation of Faster-RCNN, machine learning classifiers, and LesNet-22.

use.training: Used for training machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Light GBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) and the LesNet-22 deep learning model.

To visually analyze the performance of the model and determine the Area Under the Curve (AUC) score for efficiency evaluation.

To quantitatively evaluate the performance of Faster-RCNN for lesion detection and machine learning classifiers/Deep Learning models for classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between machine learning classifiers and the proposed LesNet-22 architecture were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2017.notes: Detection results for ISIC-2017 dataset using Faster-RCNN.

ISIC-2017_Classification.notes: LesNet-22 outperformed all machine learning classifiers. Random Forest was the best among ML classifiers with 82% accuracy.

Image resizing to 224x224 pixels before training
conclusions: The proposed LesNet-22 deep learning architecture, which combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), outperformed traditional machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Light GBM, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost) in skin lesion classification.

LesNet-22 achieved an accuracy of 94% on the ISIC-2017 dataset and 91% on the ISIC-2019 dataset, demonstrating superior performance compared to the best-performing machine learning classifier, Random Forest, which achieved 82% and 73% accuracy respectively.
The Faster-RCNN model effectively localized skin lesions, achieving detection accuracies of 97% for ISIC-2017 and 93% for ISIC-2019.
The proposed LesNet-22 model demonstrated high efficiency with Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores of 0.98 for ISIC-2017 and 0.99 for ISIC-2019, indicating strong diagnostic capability.
The integration of GrabCut for segmentation and Faster-RCNN for detection, followed by classification via LesNet-22, provides a robust computer-assisted framework for early skin cancer detection.
The study confirms that deep learning approaches can minimize the complexity of classification and avoid overfitting problems more effectively than standard machine learning methods in this context.
important_remarks: The study is limited to two public datasets (ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2019) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of LesNet-22 over baseline classifiers.
The paper does not report data leakage prevention measures or detailed train/validation/test splits, raising potential concerns about data integrity.
The Faster-RCNN detection model exhibits low recall (80% for ISIC-2017, 82% for ISIC-2019), indicating a risk of missing lesions in clinical deployment.
The proposed LesNet-22 architecture is relatively small (224x224 input, limited layers) compared to modern state-of-the-art deep learning models, potentially limiting feature extraction capacity.
The paper does not report data augmentation techniques, which may contribute to overfitting despite the use of dropout and batch normalization.
The study focuses on dermoscopic images only, limiting clinical relevance to settings where dermoscopy equipment is available.
The paper does not address class imbalance issues within the datasets, which can bias classifier performance.
